content:
# Luke Chapter 17 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 109-110 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_017_page_001.jpg, LUK_017_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And he said to his disciples, "It is impossible that stumbling-blocks should not come, but woe to him through whom they come.

**2** It would be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck and he were thrown into the sea, than that he should cause one of these little ones to stumble.

**3** Take heed to yourselves. If your brother sins, rebuke him; and if he repents, forgive him.

**4** And if seven times in the day he sins against you, and seven times turns back to you, saying, 'I repent,' you shall forgive him."

**5** And the apostles said to the Lord, "Add faith to us."

**6** And the Lord said, "If you had faith as a grain of mustard seed, you would say to this mulberry tree, 'Be uprooted and be planted in the sea,' and it would obey you.

**7** But which of you, having a servant plowing or tending sheep, will say to him when he has come in from the field, 'Come at once and recline at table'?

**8** Will he not rather say to him, 'Prepare what I may eat, and gird yourself and serve me while I eat and drink, and after these things you shall eat and drink'?

**9** Does he have gratitude toward that servant because he did the things commanded? I think not.

**10** So also you, when you have done all the things commanded you, say, 'We are unprofitable servants; we have done that which we were obligated to do.'"

**11** And it came to pass, as he was going to Jerusalem, that he was passing through the midst of Samaria and Galilee.

**12** And as he entered into a certain village, ten leprous men met him, who stood at a distance.

**13** And they lifted up their voices, saying, "Jesus, Master, have mercy on us."

**14** And having seen them, he said to them, "Go, show yourselves to the priests." And it came to pass, as they were going, they were cleansed.

**15** And one of them, having seen that he was healed, turned back, with a loud voice glorifying God,

**16** and he fell upon his face at his feet, giving thanks to him. And he was a Samaritan.

**17** And Jesus, answering, said, "Were not the ten cleansed? But where are the nine?

**18** Were none found returning to give glory to God except this foreigner?"

**19** And he said to him, "Rise and go; your faith has saved you."

**20** And having been asked by the Pharisees when the kingdom of God is coming, he answered them and said, "The kingdom of God does not come with observation;

**21** nor will they say, 'Behold, here!' or 'There!' For behold, the kingdom of God is in the midst of you."

**22** And he said to the disciples, "Days will come when you will desire to see one of the days of the Son of Man, and you will not see it.

**23** And they will say to you, 'Behold, there!' or 'Behold, here!' Do not go away, nor pursue.

**24** For as the lightning, when it flashes from one part under heaven, shines to the other part under heaven, so shall the Son of Man be in his day.

**25** But first he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation.

**26** And just as it came to pass in the days of Noah, so also shall it be in the days of the Son of Man.

**27** They were eating, they were drinking, they were marrying, they were being given in marriage, until the day when Noah entered into the ark, and the flood came and destroyed them all.

**28** Likewise, just as it came to pass in the days of Lot — they were eating, they were drinking, they were buying, they were selling, they were planting, they were building —

**29** but on the day when Lot went out from Sodom, it rained fire and brimstone from heaven and destroyed them all.

**30** According to these things shall it be on the day when the Son of Man is revealed.

**31** In that day, let him who is on the housetop, with his goods in the house, not come down to take them up; and likewise let him who is in the field not turn back to the things behind.

**32** Remember the wife of Lot.

**33** Whoever seeks to preserve his life shall lose it, and whoever loses it shall preserve it alive.

**34** I say to you, in that night there shall be two upon one bed; the one shall be taken and the other shall be left.

**35** There shall be two women grinding together; the one shall be taken and the other shall be left."

**36** [This verse is absent from Codex Sinaiticus.]

**37** And answering, they say to him, "Where, Lord?" And he said to them, "Where the body is, there also will the eagles be gathered together."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 109-110) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 16:25 through Luke 17:18 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 16:25, Lk. 17:2, Lk. 17:10, and Lk. 17:18. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 17:26 through Luke 18:14 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 17:26, Lk. 17:35, Lk. 18:7, and Lk. 18:14. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΑΝΕΝΔΕΚΤΟΝ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΤΟΥ ΤΑ ΣΚΑΝΔΑΛΑ ΜΗ ΕΛΘΕΙΝ (17:1, "it is impossible that stumbling-blocks should not come")
- ΛΙΘΟΣ ΜΥΛΙΚΟΣ (17:2, "a millstone")
- ΕΠΤΑΚΙΣ ΤΗΣ ΗΜΕΡΑΣ (17:4, "seven times in the day")
- ΠΡΟΣΘΕΣ ΗΜΙΝ ΠΙΣΤΙΝ (17:5, "add faith to us")
- ΩΣ ΚΟΚΚΟΝ ΣΙΝΑΠΕΩΣ (17:6, "as a grain of mustard seed")
- ΤΗ ΣΥΚΑΜΙΝΩ ΤΑΥΤΗ (17:6, "this mulberry tree")
- ΔΟΥΛΟΙ ΑΧΡΕΙΟΙ ΕΣΜΕΝ (17:10, "we are unprofitable servants")
- ΔΙΑ ΜΕΣΟΝ ΣΑΜΑΡΕΙΑΣ ΚΑΙ ΓΑΛΙΛΑΙΑΣ (17:11, "through the midst of Samaria and Galilee")
- ΔΕΚΑ ΛΕΠΡΟΙ ΑΝΔΡΕΣ (17:12, "ten leprous men")
- ΙΗΣΟΥ ΕΠΙΣΤΑΤΑ ΕΛΕΗΣΟΝ ΗΜΑΣ (17:13, "Jesus, Master, have mercy on us")
- ΕΝ ΤΩ ΥΠΑΓΕΙΝ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΕΚΑΘΑΡΙΣΘΗΣΑΝ (17:14, "as they were going, they were cleansed")
- ΚΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΣ ΗΝ ΣΑΜΑΡΕΙΤΗΣ (17:16, "and he was a Samaritan")
- Ο ΑΛΛΟΓΕΝΗΣ ΟΥΤΟΣ (17:18, "this foreigner")
- Η ΠΙΣΤΙΣ ΣΟΥ ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ ΣΕ (17:19, "your faith has saved you")
- ΜΕΤΑ ΠΑΡΑΤΗΡΗΣΕΩΣ (17:20, "with observation")
- ΕΝΤΟΣ ΥΜΩΝ (17:21, "in the midst of you")
- ΕΝ ΤΗ ΗΜΕΡΑ ΑΥΤΟΥ (17:24, "in his day")
- ΜΝΗΜΟΝΕΥΕΤΕ ΤΗΣ ΓΥΝΑΙΚΟΣ ΛΩΤ (17:32, "remember the wife of Lot")
- ΖΩΟΓΟΝΗΣΕΙ ΑΥΤΗΝ (17:33, "shall preserve it alive")
- Verse 36 absent from the manuscript
- ΟΠΟΥ ΤΟ ΣΩΜΑ ΕΚΕΙ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΑΕΤΟΙ ΕΠΙΣΥΝΑΧΘΗΣΟΝΤΑΙ (17:37, "where the body is, there also will the eagles be gathered together")

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **17:1 — "It is impossible that stumbling-blocks should not come" vs. "It is inevitable that things will come that cause people to stumble":** EMET renders "It is impossible [for] the stumbling-blocks not to come," LOGOS "It is impossible that stumbling-blocks should not come," PNEUMA "It is inevitable that things will come that cause people to stumble into sin." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the subjunctive construction "should not come" renders the articular infinitive naturally in formal English; EMET's bracketed preposition is unnecessary; PNEUMA paraphrases too freely, adding "into sin" and replacing "stumbling-blocks" with a clause.

2. **17:2 — "thrown" vs. "cast":** EMET renders "thrown into the sea," LOGOS "cast into the sea," PNEUMA "thrown into the sea." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "thrown" is the more natural English rendering of ΕΡΡΙΠΤΑΙ; "cast" (LOGOS) is slightly archaic but acceptable.

3. **17:3 — "Take heed" vs. "Watch yourselves":** EMET renders "Take-heed to yourselves," LOGOS "Take heed to yourselves," PNEUMA "Watch yourselves carefully." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Take heed to yourselves" renders ΠΡΟΣΕΧΕΤΕ ΕΑΥΤΟΙΣ formally and naturally; PNEUMA adds "carefully" which is not in the Greek.

4. **17:4 — "turns back" vs. "should turn back":** EMET renders "turns-back to you," LOGOS "should turn back to you," PNEUMA "comes back to you." SOPHIA follows EMET's sense without his hyphenation — "turns back" preserves the present force of ΕΠΙΣΤΡΕΨΗ in this conditional context; PNEUMA's "comes back" loses the specific turning language.

5. **17:5 — "Add faith to us" vs. "Give us more faith":** All three agree on the meaning. EMET and LOGOS render "Add faith to us," PNEUMA renders "Give us more faith!" SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "Add faith to us" is the literal rendering of ΠΡΟΣΘΕΣ ΗΜΙΝ ΠΙΣΤΙΝ; PNEUMA's paraphrase, while clear, loses the specific verb.

6. **17:6 — "as a grain of mustard seed" vs. "as a grain of mustard":** EMET renders "as a grain of mustard," LOGOS "as a grain of mustard seed," PNEUMA "even as small as a mustard seed." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "mustard seed" is the standard English rendering of ΚΟΚΚΟΝ ΣΙΝΑΠΕΩΣ; EMET's "grain of mustard" is more literal but less idiomatic in English.

7. **17:7 — "servant" vs. "slave":** EMET renders "slave" (ΔΟΥΛΟΝ), LOGOS "servant," PNEUMA "servant." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "servant" is the rendering consistent with SOPHIA's practice for ΔΟΥΛΟΣ in parabolic contexts (cf. 16:13); EMET's "slave" is more literally precise but disrupts the English register.

8. **17:7 — "tending sheep" vs. "shepherding":** EMET renders "shepherding," LOGOS "tending sheep," PNEUMA "tending sheep." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "tending sheep" renders ΠΟΙΜΑΙΝΟΝΤΑ more accessibly while preserving the meaning; "shepherding" (EMET) is acceptable but less natural.

9. **17:8 — "gird yourself" vs. "put on your apron":** EMET renders "having girded-yourself, serve me," LOGOS "gird yourself and serve me," PNEUMA "put on your apron, and serve me." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "gird yourself" preserves the formal register of ΠΕΡΙΖΩΣΑΜΕΝΟΣ; PNEUMA's "put on your apron" is interpretive.

10. **17:9 — "gratitude toward that servant" vs. "gratitude to the slave":** EMET renders "gratitude to the slave," LOGOS "gratitude toward that servant," PNEUMA "the master owe the servant special thanks." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "gratitude toward that servant" renders ΜΗ ΕΧΕΙ ΧΑΡΙΝ ΤΩ ΔΟΥΛΩ precisely; "toward" is better than "to" for ΧΑΡΙΝ with dative; PNEUMA paraphrases freely.

11. **17:10 — "unprofitable servants" vs. "unworthy servants":** EMET renders "unprofitable slaves," LOGOS "unprofitable servants," PNEUMA "unworthy servants." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "unprofitable" is the more precise rendering of ΑΧΡΕΙΟΙ (from Α + ΧΡΕΙΑ, "without use/profit"); PNEUMA's "unworthy" adds a moral dimension not present in the Greek.

12. **17:10 — "obligated" vs. "owing":** EMET renders "what we were owing to do," LOGOS "what we were obligated to do," PNEUMA "what was our duty." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "obligated" renders ΩΦΕΙΛΟΜΕΝ precisely; EMET's "owing" is acceptable but less formal; PNEUMA's "duty" paraphrases.

13. **17:11 — "it came to pass" vs. "it came-to-be":** EMET renders "it came-to-be," LOGOS "it came to pass," PNEUMA "Now as Jesus was on his way." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — consistent with the established SOPHIA rendering of ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ (cf. 16:22).

14. **17:13 — "their voices" vs. "their voice":** EMET renders "their voice" (singular, preserving ΦΩΝΗΝ), LOGOS "their voices" (plural in English), PNEUMA "in loud voices." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — English idiom naturally uses the plural "voices" for multiple speakers; EMET's singular, while textually precise, reads unnaturally.

15. **17:14 — "Go, show yourselves" vs. "Having gone, show yourselves":** EMET renders "Having gone, show yourselves," LOGOS "Go, show yourselves," PNEUMA "Go and show yourselves." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Go, show yourselves" renders ΠΟΡΕΥΘΕΝΤΕΣ ΕΠΙΔΕΙΞΑΤΕ naturally; EMET's participial construction is over-literal; PNEUMA adds a conjunction.

16. **17:17 — "But where are the nine?" vs. "But the nine — where [are they]?":** EMET renders "But the nine — where [are they]?" LOGOS "But where are the nine?" PNEUMA "Where are the other nine?" SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "But where are the nine?" is natural and preserves the adversative; EMET's dash construction, while mimicking the Greek word order, is unnecessarily dramatic; PNEUMA adds "other" which is not in the Greek.

17. **17:19 — "Rise and go" vs. "Having risen, go":** EMET renders "Having risen, go," LOGOS "Rise and go," PNEUMA "Get up and go on your way." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Rise and go" renders ΑΝΑΣΤΑΣ ΠΟΡΕΥΟΥ naturally; EMET's participial form is awkward in English; PNEUMA adds "on your way."

18. **17:19 — "has saved you" vs. "has made you well":** EMET renders "has saved you," LOGOS "has saved you," PNEUMA "has made you well." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "has saved you" preserves the full theological weight of ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ; PNEUMA's "has made you well" reduces ΣΩΖΩ to physical healing, which all three translators agree is inadequate to the context.

19. **17:21 — "in the midst of you" vs. "within you" vs. "among you":** EMET renders "within you," LOGOS "in the midst of you," PNEUMA "among you." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "in the midst of you" renders ΕΝΤΟΣ ΥΜΩΝ with the spatial sense appropriate to the Pharisaic audience; all three translators note in their commentary that the interior reading ("within you") is less likely given the addressees; LOGOS's rendering preserves the ambiguity most faithfully.

20. **17:24 — "as the lightning, when it flashes" vs. "just-as the lightning, flashing":** EMET renders "just-as the lightning, flashing from the [one part] under heaven, shines to the [other part] under heaven," LOGOS "as the lightning, when it flashes out of one part under heaven, shines to the other part under heaven," PNEUMA "just as lightning flashes and lights up the entire sky from one horizon to the other." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the temporal clause "when it flashes" renders the participial ΑΣΤΡΑΠΤΟΥΣΑ naturally; EMET's brackets and hyphenation clutter the verse; PNEUMA paraphrases "horizon to horizon" which is not in the Greek.

21. **17:25 — "he must suffer" vs. "it is necessary [for] him to suffer":** EMET renders "it is necessary [for] him to suffer many [things]," LOGOS "he must suffer many things," PNEUMA "he must suffer terribly." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "he must" renders ΔΕΙ ΑΥΤΟΝ naturally; PNEUMA's "terribly" is interpretive, replacing ΠΟΛΛΑ ("many things") with an adverb.

22. **17:29 — "brimstone" vs. "sulfur":** EMET renders "brimstone," LOGOS "brimstone," PNEUMA "sulfur." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "brimstone" is the traditional rendering of ΘΕΙΟΝ in biblical contexts and maintains the formal register.

23. **17:30 — "According to these things" vs. "It will be just like that":** EMET renders "According-to the same [things]," LOGOS "According to these things," PNEUMA "It will be just like that." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "According to these things" renders ΚΑΤΑ ΤΑ ΑΥΤΑ formally; PNEUMA is too colloquial.

24. **17:33 — "preserve his life" vs. "preserve his soul":** EMET renders "preserve his soul," LOGOS "preserve his life," PNEUMA "preserve his life." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "life" renders ΨΥΧΗ in context more accurately for English readers; "soul" (EMET) introduces a dualistic connotation not intended.

25. **17:33 — "shall preserve it alive" vs. "will bring-it-to-life":** EMET renders "will bring-it-to-life," LOGOS "shall preserve it alive," PNEUMA "will keep it alive." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "shall preserve it alive" renders ΖΩΟΓΟΝΗΣΕΙ precisely, capturing both the preservation and the life-giving aspects of the verb; EMET's paraphrase is acceptable but less natural.

26. **17:34 — "in that night" vs. "in this night":** EMET renders "in this night" (ΤΑΥΤΗ ΤΗ ΝΥΚΤΙ), LOGOS "in that night," PNEUMA "on that night." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "in that night" is the better contextual rendering, since Jesus speaks of a future eschatological event; the deictic ΤΑΥΤΗ functions as a demonstrative pointing forward, rendered "that" in English narrative.

27. **17:37 — "eagles" vs. "vultures":** EMET renders "eagles," LOGOS "eagles," PNEUMA "vultures." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "eagles" is the standard rendering of ΑΕΤΟΙ; while all three translators note in their commentary that vultures are the more likely zoological referent, "eagles" preserves the literal Greek term, with the zoological note belonging to commentary rather than translation.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
